pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: Further history revealed that Ms. E.L. had recently been prescribed a new medication for her chronic, mild anxiety—a barbiturate sleep aid—shortly before the onset of her abdominal pain. Barbiturates are potent inducers of the P450 system and are highly porphyrogenic, triggering the acute attack. The diagnostic key was the elevated urinary porphyrin precursors (Porphobilinogen and delta-Aminolevulinic acid). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a deficiency in the enzyme Porphobilinogen deaminase (PBGD), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. Normally, this deficiency is compensated for, but during acute crises (triggered by certain drugs, fasting, or hormones), the enzyme requirements outstrip production. This leads to the accumulation of the toxic heme precursors, delta-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and Porphobilinogen (PBG). These accumulated neurotoxins are responsible for the severe neurological, psychiatric, and autonomic symptoms (hypertension, tachycardia, ascending neuropathy, and pain). The severe hyponatremia is often attributable to central effects causing inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH). The characteristic darkening of urine upon exposure to light is due to the non-enzymatic oxidation of excess PBG. Treatment requires immediate cessation of the inciting agent and administration of intravenous hemin (Panhematin), which suppresses the production of ALA synthase, thereby reducing the toxic precursors.
